title: Impeller (Rendering Engine)
objectives: Understand Impeller via a real-life analogy
Know why Flutter moved from Skia to Impeller
Enable / disable Impeller and diagnose issues

sections.markdown:
**Think of a restaurant that pre-preps its sauces vs one that makes each sauce from scratch when you order.**

- **Skia (the old renderer)** was the from-scratch restaurant. The first time you ordered pasta with cream sauce, the chef opened flour, cream, butter, and *cooked the sauce right then* — the wait was noticeable ('shader compilation jank'). Once cooked, subsequent orders were fast. But every dish combination triggered another first-time cook.
- **Impeller** is the pre-prep restaurant. Every sauce is made ahead of time, kept warm, and portioned. When you order, it's on the plate in 30 seconds — every time, first time. No first-run stall.
- **Same ingredients** (pixels, geometry, GPU); different **kitchen strategy**. The pre-prep costs more space (larger app bundle) but delivers **predictable, jank-free performance from frame one**.

**Why this matters:** every senior Flutter interview in 2024–25 asks about Impeller. It's the biggest internal shift since Flutter 1.0. Understanding it separates 'I use Flutter' from 'I know Flutter'.

**Impeller** is Flutter's modern rendering engine (Flutter 3.10+; default on iOS since 3.10, default on Android since Flutter 3.16 / 3.24 on capable devices). It replaces Skia for most Flutter apps. Key idea: **precompile all shaders at build time** into the app bundle, eliminating first-frame shader-compilation jank. Written from scratch by the Flutter team; talks directly to Metal (iOS/macOS), Vulkan (Android/Linux/Windows), or OpenGL ES (fallback).
## Skia vs Impeller — the real differences

rows: **JIT at runtime** — first use of a shader stalls the raster thread (~100–300 ms)
**Precompiled at build time** — zero first-use stalls
OpenGL ES, Metal, Vulkan (via Skia's abstraction)
Direct Metal / Vulkan / OpenGL ES — one thin abstraction
New; some edge-case glyphs / effects still catching up

sections.markdown: **The killer insight:** Skia was designed for a browser environment where shaders come and go dynamically. Flutter, though, has a *fixed* set of visual primitives per build. Impeller exploits that — precompute everything possible at build time. It's not that Skia is 'bad', it's that Flutter finally has a graphics engine designed *for Flutter's constraints*.
## What Impeller fixed — the first-run jank story

sections.code:
Before Impeller (Skia era):

  User taps a button first time  →
    Flutter records draw commands  →
      Raster thread sees a new shader combination it hasn't compiled  →
        [~150 ms shader compile on the raster thread]  →
          Rasterize + present
  → USER SEES: 'the app hitched for a moment'

  Same tap second time  →  cached shader  →  no hitch

With Impeller:

  User taps button first time  →
    Flutter records draw commands  →
      Raster thread uses precompiled shader  →
        Rasterize + present
  → USER SEES: smooth

The fix: shift shader compilation from raster-thread runtime to build time.

sections.markdown: ## Sample program — check + toggle Impeller
## Common misconceptions
**Misconception 1: 'Impeller is a graphics library.'** No — it's Flutter's OWN rendering engine, purpose-built for Flutter's needs. It talks directly to Metal / Vulkan / OpenGL ES.
**Misconception 2: 'You can write custom shaders differently in Impeller.'** Almost the same API (`FragmentProgram`), but the toolchain compiles them ahead of time. Same GLSL-like syntax as before.
**Mistake 3: Disabling Impeller in production 'because it's new'.** Modern Impeller has been stable on iOS for years and on Android since 3.16+. Disabling brings back first-run jank.
**Misconception 4: 'Skia is deprecated.'** Skia still ships as the fallback and is used on some platforms/paths. Google keeps investing in both, but Impeller is the future for Flutter.
## Best practices
**1. Use the default renderer.** Modern iOS + Android = Impeller. Don't turn it off unless you're diagnosing a specific bug.
**2. Report edge-case bugs.** Impeller occasionally has glyph or blur bugs on niche devices — file on the Flutter engine tracker; a fix usually lands quickly.
**3. Combine with Baseline Profiles on Android.** Impeller kills shader jank; Baseline Profiles kill first-launch AOT-warmup jank. Both together = smooth cold start.
**4. Test on OpenGL ES fallback devices.** Older Android hardware without Vulkan falls back to OpenGL — verify your custom shaders still render correctly.
**5. Use `FragmentProgram` for GPU effects.** Impeller-compatible; blazing fast for per-pixel work like blur, distortion, gradients.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Toggle Impeller.** Run your app twice: once with `--enable-impeller`, once with `--no-enable-impeller`. Note the log line. Feel the first-run difference.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Complex animation first-run.** Build a heavy animation (blur + gradient + rotation). Run with Skia — measure first-frame timing. Run with Impeller — measure again.
**Practice 3 (Hard) — Custom FragmentProgram shader.** Write a simple SPIR-V shader that gradients based on distance from center.
**Practice 4 (Expert) — Compare on an older Android device.** Run on Android 8 or lower where Vulkan may be missing — observe OpenGL fallback behavior.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is Impeller and why did Flutter build it?**

**A:** Impeller is Flutter's modern rendering engine, built to replace Skia for most Flutter apps. **Why:** Skia compiled shaders JIT at runtime — the first time a novel shader combination was used, the raster thread would stall 100–300 ms compiling it, causing infamous 'first-run jank' especially on iOS. Impeller **precompiles all shaders at build time** into the app bundle, so the first frame is as fast as the millionth. Also: Impeller talks directly to Metal / Vulkan / OpenGL ES (via a thin abstraction), giving Flutter tighter control over the GPU pipeline. The tradeoff is a slightly larger app bundle. Impeller is default on iOS (since Flutter 3.10) and on modern Android (since 3.16+).

**Q2: What was 'shader compilation jank' and how did Impeller fix it?**

**A:** Skia's rendering pipeline uses shader programs (small GPU programs) to draw complex effects like gradients, shadows, blurs, and transitions. Skia compiled these lazily — on first use, the compilation ran on the raster thread. On iOS especially, this could take 100–300 ms, causing a very visible hitch when the user triggered an animation for the first time. **Impeller's fix:** precompile every shader Flutter will ever need at BUILD time, ship the compiled bytecode inside the app bundle. At runtime, shaders are just loaded — no compilation. Result: consistent frame times from the very first frame. It's the single biggest UX win Impeller delivers.

**Q3: Impeller vs Skia — what are the trade-offs?**

**A:** **Impeller pros:** no shader jank, tighter Metal/Vulkan control, purpose-built for Flutter's needs, forward-looking (Google's future investment). **Impeller cons:** larger app bundle (precompiled shaders), a few edge-case rendering differences from Skia (glyphs, some blurs) that occasionally need fixes, newer so less battle-tested outside Flutter. **Skia pros:** 20+ years of maturity, powers Chrome, thoroughly tested across every hardware combo. **Skia cons:** the shader-jank problem is inherent to its design. **Rule:** default to Impeller. Switch to Skia (`--no-enable-impeller`) only when diagnosing a specific rendering bug you've confirmed is Impeller-only.

**Q4: How do you enable or disable Impeller?**

**A:** **Enable/disable at build/run time via CLI flags:** `flutter run --enable-impeller` or `flutter run --no-enable-impeller`. **In the manifest (Android):** `<meta-data android:name="io.flutter.embedding.android.EnableImpeller" android:value="true"/>`. **In Info.plist (iOS):** `FLTEnableImpeller = YES / NO`. **Verify:** run with `-v` and watch for 'Using Impeller' vs 'Using Skia' in the initial engine log. Modern Flutter versions default to Impeller on iOS + modern Android — you don't need to enable it unless you're testing.

**Q5: Does Impeller change how you write Flutter code?**

**A:** Almost never. The Dart-side APIs (`CustomPainter`, `Canvas`, `Paint`, `Path`, `ImageFilter`) are identical. **Custom `FragmentProgram` shaders** use the same GLSL-like syntax as before, but the toolchain now precompiles them to SPIR-V and bundles them. **Edge cases:** some very old Skia hacks (specific `saveLayer` blend modes, exotic path operations) may render slightly differently — but for 99% of app code, Impeller is a drop-in replacement. **Rule:** write normal Flutter code; Impeller is an engine-level detail invisible to your Dart source.

**Q6: What is a `FragmentProgram` and how does it work with Impeller?**

**A:** A `FragmentProgram` is a GPU fragment shader you write for per-pixel effects (blurs, distortions, gradients, procedural graphics). You write it in a Flutter-flavored GLSL, save it as a `.frag` file, and declare it in `pubspec.yaml`. At build time, the Flutter tool compiles it to SPIR-V (Impeller's IR) and bundles it. At runtime you load it with `FragmentProgram.fromAsset('shaders/foo.frag')`, get a `FragmentShader`, set uniforms, and draw with `Paint()..shader = fragmentShader`. **With Impeller:** shaders are precompiled — zero first-run jank. **Use cases:** the reason to reach for this over `CustomPainter` is pure GPU speed for per-pixel work — thousands of times faster for large effects.

**Q7: What platforms does Impeller support?**

**A:** **iOS + macOS**: Impeller uses **Metal**. Default since Flutter 3.10 (2023). **Android**: Impeller uses **Vulkan** on capable devices (Android 10+ with Vulkan 1.1), falls back to **OpenGL ES** on older devices. Default on capable devices since Flutter 3.16 (2023), broader default since 3.24 (2024). **Windows / Linux**: Impeller support is progressing; currently Skia is still default on most desktops. **Flutter Web**: does NOT use Impeller — Web uses CanvasKit (Skia compiled to WebAssembly) or the HTML renderer. **Rule of thumb:** if you build for mobile in 2025, you're on Impeller by default.

## Real-world usage

- **Every modern mobile Flutter app** — Impeller is the default renderer.
- **BMW MyBMW, Google Ads, Google Pay, Alibaba** — all run on Impeller now.
- **Impeller Playground** (community) — a testing app showing per-primitive rendering behavior.
- **Custom shaders in production** — Rive, Lottie, `flutter_shaders` package all work with Impeller.
- **Older Android devices without Vulkan** — silent OpenGL ES fallback; works, marginally slower.
- **Fallback to Skia** — sometimes recommended for very specific enterprise devices with known Impeller issues; usually resolved by upgrading Flutter.

## Advanced notes

- **`impellerc`** — Impeller's shader compiler; part of the Flutter tool.
- **SPIR-V** — the intermediate representation for GPU code Impeller emits.
- **`FragmentProgram.compile()`** — runtime compile fallback (avoid).
- **`ImageFilter.blur`** — much smoother on Impeller than Skia.
- **`Canvas.saveLayer`** — was very expensive on Skia; Impeller mitigates but still not free.
- **Impeller's own DevTools tab** — inspects layer trees and rasterization details.
- **Impeller-only features** — some new rendering effects may ship Impeller-first (with a Skia fallback).
- **Vulkan on Android** — Impeller requires Vulkan 1.1 for the fast path; falls back to OpenGL ES 3 otherwise.
- **`Rive Renderer`** — an alternative built on top of Impeller for interactive vector animations.
- **Testing on both renderers** — CI matrices sometimes include `--no-enable-impeller` runs to catch regressions.

flashcards.back: Flutter's modern rendering engine — replaces Skia; precompiles shaders at build time.
No first-run shader-compilation jank — same shader is precompiled and bundled.
iOS (since 3.10), modern Android (since 3.16+). Not Web (which uses CanvasKit).
Slightly larger app bundle (bundled shader binaries).
flutter run --enable-impeller / --no-enable-impeller; also via AndroidManifest / Info.plist meta.

options: Precompiled at build time → zero first-run jank

quiz.q: Does Impeller change Dart-side rendering API?
quiz.explain: Engine-level change; invisible to app code.

challenges.text: Run your app twice: with and without --enable-impeller; note the log line.
Read Info.plist / AndroidManifest to find where Impeller is toggled.
Build a first-time-complex animation; measure first-frame time on both renderers.
Add ImageFilter.blur to a scrollable; feel the smoothness on Impeller.
Write a small .frag FragmentProgram (radial gradient); render via Paint().shader.
Compare app-bundle size before/after enabling Impeller.
On older Android (no Vulkan), force OpenGL ES fallback and profile the perf gap.
